title: Fungal and Protozoal Infections of the Neonate
authors: Kaufman DA, Nadipuram SM, Manzoni P / Fanaroff & Martin 2025

blocks.text:
ทำไม neonatologist ต้องเชี่ยวชาญ Fungal Infections?
(1) ICI ใน ELBW (<1000 g): incidence ~10–24% ถ้าไม่มี prophylaxis — mortality 26–50% ขึ้นกับ site; (2) 73% ของ ELBW ที่ติด Candida: ตายหรือรอดพร้อม neurodevelopmental impairment (Benjamin Pediatrics 2006); (3) Fluconazole prophylaxis ลด ICI ได้ 80–90% + ลด Candida-related mortality 96% (Cochrane 2015); (4) Line removal ภายใน 24 ชั่วโมง: ลด mortality จาก 37% → 21% (NICHD NRN data, Benjamin 2006); (5) Congenital toxoplasmosis: 1–3/1000 live births US — untreated → chorioretinitis, hydrocephalus, mental retardation. เราป้องกัน, วินิจฉัย, และรักษาได้ — นี่คือ preventable mortality ที่แท้จริง.
เป้าหมายการเรียนรู้วันนี้
อธิบาย pathogenesis ของ invasive Candida infection (ICI): 4 steps — adhesion, colonization, dissemination, abscess formation
ระบุ risk factors ที่สำคัญที่สุดสำหรับ ICI ใน NICU พร้อม specific OR และ odds ของ progression จาก colonization สู่ infection
วินิจฉัย Candida cutaneous candidiasis (congenital vs late-onset), Candida BSI, UTI, CNS infection, peritonitis — evaluation workup และ treatment duration แต่ละ type
อธิบาย end-organ dissemination (EOD) screening: ทำเมื่อไหร่ ทำอะไร — echo, renal US, HUS/MRI, ophthalmology
เปรียบเทียบ antifungals: amphotericin B deoxycholate vs lipid vs fluconazole vs micafungin — dose, mechanism, ข้อดีข้อเสียใน NICU
อธิบาย evidence ของ fluconazole prophylaxis: RCT สำคัญ, efficacy %, dosing schedule, safety ระยะยาว, azole resistance data
เปรียบเทียบ fluconazole vs nystatin prophylaxis: efficacy, ข้อจำกัด, ต้นทุน, NICU applicability
ระบุ non-Candida fungi ใน NICU: Aspergillus, Mucormycosis, Malassezia, Trichosporon — presentation, treatment
วินิจฉัยและจัดการ congenital malaria, PJP, และ congenital toxoplasmosis — clinical features, diagnosis, treatment
ระบุ infection control bundle สำหรับ ICI prevention: antifungal stewardship, antibiotic stewardship, CLABSI bundle, medication stewardship
1. Invasive Candida Infections — Epidemiology & Microbiology
1.1 Overview
ICI in NICU: Candida species 80–90% (C. albicans + C. parapsilosis); Aspergillus, Mucormycosis, Malassezia, Trichosporon rare แต่ severe
Two main high-risk populations: (1) ELBW premature infant; (2) NICU patients with complex GI disease (NEC, gastroschisis, spontaneous bowel perforation)
Incidence trend: เพิ่มใน 1980s–1990s ตาม survival ของ ELBW; ลดลงหลัง 2001 เนื่องจาก antifungal prophylaxis + antibiotic stewardship + CLABSI bundles
1.2 Incidence by Gestational Age / Birth Weight

ICI Incidence — NICHD Neonatal Research Network Data
Benjamin et al Pediatrics 2010 (128 US NICUs, n=130,523): Infants <1000 g (without prophylaxis): ~10% ICI overall; 24% ที่ 23 wk GA; 3% ที่ 28 wk GA. BSI alone vs all ICI: burden of ALL ICIs (BSI+UTI+meningitis+peritonitis) = nearly TWICE BSI rate alone. Candida BSI rate: 7.5% median for <1000 g; ↑ 1000–1500 g: 1.32%; >2500 g: 0.29%. Mortality by site: BSI 28%; UTI 26%; meningitis+peritonitis 50%; 2+ sites 57%. Overall mortality 34% vs 14% without ICI.
C. albicans: more virulent; associated mortality 43% vs 19% for C. parapsilosis in <1000 g (Benjamin 2010)
Non-albicans species ที่เพิ่มขึ้นหลัง fluconazole prophylaxis: C. glabrata, C. tropicalis — fluconazole prophylaxis virtually eliminates virulent C. albicans
1.3 Special Populations
NEC + complex GI disease: candidemia 10–16.5% ใน NEC; peritoneal Candida ใน 50% focal bowel perforation + 15% surgical NEC (Coates et al Pediatrics 2005)
Congenital heart disease: Candida = 9% of BSIs; rate 6.3/1000 admissions; mortality 21% (Ascher et al Early Hum Dev 2012)
ECMO: Candida = 2nd most common infection; risk 2–4× higher after 8–14 days vs first 7 days on ECMO (Bizzarro et al Pediatr Crit Care Med 2011)
2. Pathogenesis & Colonization
2.1 Four Steps of Invasion
Step 1 — Adhesion: surface glycoproteins (INT1p binds β-integrins on endothelium); biofilm formation → shields Candida from antifungals + host response
Step 2 — Colonization: skin, GI, respiratory tracts; 26.7–62.5% of VLBW infants colonized in first weeks (vs 10% term); most by 2–3 weeks of life
Step 3 — Dissemination: immature neutrophils, ↓ complement C3, ↓ macrophage function; C. albicans hyphae escape phagocytosis by size; candidal proteinases attack immature stratum corneum
Step 4 — Abscess formation: heart, kidneys, CNS, eyes, lungs, joints, peritoneum; each day of candidemia ↑ EOD risk by 10% (Le et al Antimicrob Agents Chemother 2013)

C. albicans morphology switch: yeast → hyphae → pseudohyphae; wild-type strain: 92% mouse mortality; constitutively filamentous: 0% — yeast form ↑ adherence + tissue invasion; hyphal form ↑ parenchymal destruction
2.2 Vertical vs Horizontal Transmission
Vertical: vaginal colonization 25–50% pregnant women (90% C. albicans); infant exposed via birth canal; genotyping confirms vertical transmission in many early cases
Horizontal: hands of HCW — 29% had Candida on hands; C. parapsilosis dominant on HCW hands (19%); C. albicans dominant in patients (14%) — wash hands!
Environmental contamination rare — Candida not isolated from incubators, wash basins, ventilators, radiant warmers, or catheters in most studies
2.3 Colonization Odds → Infection
3. Risk Factors สำหรับ ICI
3.1 Patient-Related Risk Factors (Table 51.1)
Gestational age: strongest predictor — linear relationship; <25 wk > 25–27 wk > 28 wk (Fig 51.3 NICHD NRN data) — ไม่ใช่ birth weight อย่างเดียว

Medications ↑ fungal growth: 3rd–4th gen cephalosporins + carbapenems (bile accumulation → ↓ GI flora → ↑ Candida); H2 antagonists + PPIs; postnatal dexamethasone (animal study: dexa + antibiotics → ↑ colonization AND dissemination)
Medications ↓ immune defense: postnatal steroids; H2 antagonists; acid suppression
Conditions: NEC; focal bowel perforation; gastroschisis; prior BSI
Other risk factors: CVC; intubation; lack of enteral feedings by DOL3 (8.7% vs 3.4% Candida BSI/meningitis in NICHD NRN); hyperglycemia; male sex; vaginal delivery
Day-3 prediction (Benjamin 2010): lack of enteral feedings + 3rd-gen cephalosporin use at DOL3 → ↑ risk ICI in <1000 g — ข้อมูลนี้ guide timing ของ prophylaxis
4. Diagnosis — Cultures, BDG, EOD Screening
4.1 Cultures
Standard: blood, urine (≥10,000 CFU/mL by sterile cath; ≥1000 CFU/mL by suprapubic), CSF, peritoneal fluid at time of sepsis evaluation
Blood culture timing: Candida detected at mean 37±14 hours; 97% positive by 72 hours (Schelonka & Moser J Pediatr 2003)
Lower CFU candiduria (not meeting threshold) = high-risk colonization site → empiric antifungal + repeat culture
4.2 (1→3)-Beta-D-Glucan (BDG)
BDG cutoff: >125 pg/mL ใน neonates (vs >80 pg/mL adults) เพราะ colonization + CoNS infections raise baseline (Goudjil et al J Matern Fetal Neonatal Med 2013)
BDG ใน ICI: 364 pg/mL vs 89 pg/mL non-infected; ลดลงต่อ antifungal therapy → ใช้ monitor treatment response
False positive: blood transfusion (PRBC, FFP) → ↑ BDG ใน 133 VLBWs: 170 vs 57 pg/mL (P<0.001) — interpret cautiously post-transfusion
Mannan antigen in ET aspirate: pre-emptive treatment เมื่อ mannan ≥0.5 ng/mL → ↓ ICI จาก 75% → 0% ใน tracheal Candida-positive group (Posteraro et al Pediatr Infect Dis J 2010)
4.3 End-Organ Dissemination (EOD) Screening

EOD Screen — Algorithm (Fig 51.6)
Standard EOD screen: echocardiogram + renal US + head US + indirect ophthalmoscopy. Timing: at presentation OR after 5–7 days of appropriate antifungal therapy. If bowel disease (NEC/focal perforation): complete abdominal US to rule out liver/splenic/peritoneal involvement. Persistent candidemia >5–7 days: also add: US at previous CVC tip location (infected thrombus), complete abdominal US for abscesses, head US/MRI. Joint/bone signs (swelling, immobility): joint aspiration + MRI — do NOT rely on negative bone scan to rule out. CNS: 50% meningitis occurs WITHOUT candidemia — always do LP with sepsis evaluation; culture CSF before starting antifungals.
5. Clinical Manifestations of ICI
5.1 Congenital Cutaneous Candidiasis (CCC)
Presentation: birth to day 7 — maculopapular, papulopustular, desquamating, erythematous rash; white plaques on umbilical cord; placental pathology with yeast invasion (silver stain)
Dissemination risk: 11% term; 33% 1000–2500 g; 66% <1000 g (Darmstadt et al Pediatrics 2000) — ยิ่ง preterm ยิ่ง disseminate มาก → treat ALL cases systemically

Kaufman et al Clin Infect Dis 2017: delaying systemic treatment, using topical nystatin only, or treating <10 days → ↑ dissemination — systemic antifungal ≥14 days required; start empirically at rash presentation
Evaluation: skin culture (2 sites aerobic), blood + CSF (unless rash covers back) + urine (if >72h); send cord/placenta for pathology/silver stain
5.2 Late Cutaneous Candidiasis
After 7 days of life: similar skin manifestations; incidence up to 8% in VLBW without prophylaxis; risk factors: extreme prematurity, vaginal birth, postnatal steroids, hyperglycemia
Invasive skin infection of preterm skin → treat systemically ≥14 days; do not use topical alone
5.3 Candidemia — Clinical Signs

rows: Thrombocytopenia <100,000/μL (84%); I:T ratio ≥0.2 (77%); BDG >125 pg/mL; apnea/bradycardia; ↑ O2 requirement; assisted ventilation
Lethargy/hypotonia; GI symptoms (gastric aspirates, distention, bloody stools)

blocks.text: 5.4 Urinary Tract Infection (UTI)
Candida UTI: ~3–4% of ELBW without prophylaxis; ascending route more common than hematogenous; mortality 26% (similar to BSI 28%) in ELBWs (Benjamin 2010)
Renal abscess: 12–42% of candiduria; renal US at presentation + repeat if persistent candiduria; initial normal US ≠ no abscess (can develop 8–39 days later)
Treatment: ≥14 days; document clearance with follow-up urine culture
5.5 CNS Infection
Meningitis: 50% ของ meningitis cases เกิดโดยไม่มี candidemia — ต้องทำ LP ทุกครั้งที่ evaluate sepsis ก่อน start antifungals; CSF cell counts อาจ normal ใน early infection
CNS abscess: microscopic → not detectable by US in 7/13 cases (autopsy-proven); ALL fungal sepsis cases ต้อง HUS/MRI — ไม่ว่า CSF จะ normal หรือไม่
ICI + periventricular leukomalacia: cytokine release → white matter injury — neurodevelopmental impairment 57% vs 29% non-infected (Benjamin 2006)
5.6 GI — Peritonitis
NEC + focal bowel perforation: Candida peritonitis ใน 15% surgical NEC; 44% focal bowel perforation (Coates et al Pediatrics 2005)
Candidemia complicates NEC ใน 10–16.5% of cases
Persistent leukopenia + severe thrombocytopenia + hypotension + low UO despite treatment → consider exploratory laparotomy even if imaging negative
6. Treatment of ICI
6.1 Antifungal Drug Reference

rows: FIRST CHOICE — excellent coverage, low cost, safe in neonates; no test dose needed; preferred for UTI/renal (↑ kidney conc)
95% eradication; ↓ nephrotoxicity vs deoxycholate in older patients but comparable safety in neonates; higher cost
PK data: 12 mg/kg optimizes levels (Wade et al AAC 2008); reserve for prophylaxis primarily; azole inhibits CYP (LFT monitor)
Only echinocandin licensed for neonates in Europe; RCT vs ampho B deoxycholate: similar efficacy/safety (Benjamin et al PIDJ 2018); 10 mg/kg needed for CNS penetration
Monitor electrolytes, Ca, PO4, LFTs; dosing still being studied
FIRST LINE for Aspergillus; monitor troughs (goal 2–6 mcg/mL); protein-bound → accumulates in renal insufficiency; LFT monitor

blocks.text: 6.2 Mechanism — Amphotericin B vs Echinocandins vs Azoles
Amphotericin B: binds ergosterol → membrane pores → cell death; deoxycholate = water-soluble form; 90% protein-bound in blood; accumulates in RES (liver/spleen)
Azoles (fluconazole, voriconazole): inhibit C-14 lanosterol demethylase → ↓ ergosterol synthesis; fluconazole resistance via efflux pumps (MDR/CDR) or enzyme mutations

Echinocandins (micafungin, caspofungin, anidulafungin): non-competitive inhibitors of (1,3)-β-D-glucan synthase → ↓ cell wall glucan → fungal cell death; active against biofilms; broad Candida + Aspergillus coverage
When fluconazole is used for prophylaxis → use different antifungal (amphotericin B) for treatment to prevent resistance pressure
6.3 Central Line Removal — Critical

Line Removal: ลด Mortality จาก 37% → 21%
NICHD NRN (Benjamin Pediatrics 2006): prompt CVC removal (<24h) with Candida BSI: mortality 21% vs 37% delayed removal (P=0.024). C. parapsilosis: prompt removal → 10% mortality vs 31% delayed. Catheter replacement vs removal: catheter REMOVAL recommended — Candida biofilm on catheter → antifungals cannot penetrate. New CVC สามารถใส่ได้เมื่อ: 3+ negative cultures in first 3 days of therapy OR 2+ negative after day 4. Combination therapy: Linder et al: monotherapy sterilization 67% → 96% with polytherapy (2nd agent = fluconazole) — consider when CNS disease, disseminated ICI, or persistent candidemia >7 days.
6.4 Treatment Duration
Clearance documentation: serial blood cultures until 2+ negative after day 4 of treatment + line removal; median clearance time = 3 days with prompt line removal (2 days faster vs delayed)
Persistent candidemia >5–7 days: re-evaluate EOD; add second antifungal; consult pediatric ID
7. Prevention — Antifungal Prophylaxis
7.1 Fluconazole Prophylaxis — Evidence

Fluconazole Prophylaxis: 20+ Studies, >6400 Neonates
Overall efficacy: ↓ ICI 80% in all studies; ↓ Candida-related mortality 96% (Kaufman NeoReviews 2011). First RCT (Kaufman NEJM 2001): 100 infants <1000 g; fluconazole vs placebo; 0% vs 20% ICI (P=0.008). Multicenter RCT (Manzoni NEJM 2007, n=322 <1500 g): fluconazole 3 mg/kg vs 6 mg/kg vs placebo; colonization ↓ 29.2% → 7.7%/9.8%; ICI ↓ 13.2% → 3.8%/2.7% — both doses equal efficacy. Patient-level meta-analysis (Ericson et al Clin Infect Dis 2016): ↓ ICI or death 52%; ↓ ICI 80%; ↓ colonization 72%; 73% of patients <750 g — most extreme preterm covered. Cochrane (Cleminson 2015): confirmed efficacy; NNT ~10 for <1000 g (all ICIs).
Dosing: 3 or 6 mg/kg IV twice weekly (same 2 days, same time e.g. Tue + Fri); first dose at birth; continue while IV access present (central or peripheral)
Duration: until IV access no longer needed; for infants on full enteral feeds but still intubated → some studies continue enteral fluconazole until extubated (up to 6 weeks)

Safety — neurodevelopment: 8–10 year follow-up (Kaufman et al J Pediatr 2011): no difference in Vineland Adaptive Behavior Scales, communication, daily living, socialization, motor, quality of life, school satisfaction

Safety — azole resistance: 20-year data: no significant resistance emergence with twice-weekly 3 mg/kg; Sarvikivi et al (20-year): 2 resistant strains appeared only when NICU switched to daily 6–12 mg/kg for all patients
Resistance prevention rules: (1) twice-weekly dosing; (2) treat ICI with amphotericin B, not fluconazole; (3) limit duration to high-risk period
7.2 Nystatin Prophylaxis — Evidence
Level A-1 RCT evidence: Aydemir et al Arch Dis Child 2011 (3-arm, n=278 <1500g): nystatin 4.3% vs fluconazole 3.2% vs placebo 16.5% ICI (P<0.0001)
Efficacy: 50–60% (vs 80–90% fluconazole); UK retrospective (1459 infants): ↓ ICI 56% (4.1% → 1.8%) + ↓ all-cause mortality 17.8% → 11.8% (P<0.0001)

Limitations: cannot give if GI disease/ileus/hemodynamic instability (excluded 1/3 eligible patients in one study); oral suspension osmolarity 3002 mOsm/L (NEC concern); dosed 3–4×/day; few data <750 g + <25 wk
Cost: nystatin ~twice as expensive as fluconazole for a full course: nystatin 4×/day ×4 weeks = $112 vs fluconazole 2×/week ×4 weeks = $64.88 (US 2022 prices)
7.3 Fluconazole vs Nystatin Comparison
7.4 Targeted Prophylaxis — When to Start
High-risk targets: BW <1000 g AND/OR ≤27 wk GA (ICI incidence ≥5%, mortality ≥20%)
Moderate-risk: 750–999 g or 26–27 wk WITH CVC + PN + additional risk factors (vaginal birth, >2d antibiotics in week 1, >3 days no enteral feeding, 3rd/4th gen ceph/carbapenem, complex GI disease)

Other high-risk situations: treatment with 3rd/4th gen ceph or carbapenems (cover while receiving); NEC or spontaneous intestinal perforation (cover until no IV access); gastroschisis/Hirschsprung (start at birth, continue until no IV needed)
Timing: start ≤72h after birth — starting after colonization 30% less effective (Ozturk et al Mycoses 2006); Manzoni 2007: ↓ efficacy if already colonized
8. Non-Candida Fungal Infections
8.1 Aspergillus vs Mucormycosis — Comparison

rows: Small white powder-like → yellow crusted → target lesions → dark scablike
Erythema + induration → necrosis → BLACK ESCHAR at covered area/IV site/local trauma
Dichotomously branched, SEPTATE — 10% KOH / GMS stain
Hospital construction dust, ventilation systems, soil
Adhesive tape, monitor leads, NICU securement devices
Amphotericin B + surgical debridement; posaconazole (future study)
Mucormycosis RESISTANT to azoles (treat with ampho B)

blocks.text: Aspergillus prevention: clean NICU ventilation systems regularly; containment during hospital construction — spores inhaled → pulmonary/cutaneous/disseminated disease
Mucormycosis survival: improved from 18% (pre-2000) → 64% (post-2000) with early diagnosis + ampho B + surgical debridement — early recognition is key
8.2 Malassezia furfur / M. pachydermatis

M. furfur: lipid-dependent; colonizes skin + GI + intralipid; BSI in lowest BW/longest stay infants; treatment: stop intralipid + remove CVC — often sufficient; systemic amphotericin B if significant symptomatology
M. pachydermatis: outbreak-prone; source = HCW/family/dogs; education + hand washing → eradicates from NICU
Trichosporon: outbreak prone; ↑ mortality in ELBW; susceptible to amphotericin B; education + hand washing effective for elimination
8.3 Other Rare Fungi

Coccidioidomycosis: endemic in SW US/Mexico/South America; pneumonia in neonates (vs asymptomatic adults); vertical transmission possible (onset <1 week); treat ALL neonatal cases (↑ dissemination risk) — amphotericin B initial → fluconazole oral to complete
Cryptococcosis: Cryptococcus neoformans; soil + pigeon excreta; ↑ immunocompromised; extremely rare in newborns; India ink CSF prep; treat with amphotericin B + flucytosine/fluconazole

Histoplasmosis: Histoplasma capsulatum; Ohio/Mississippi River valleys; thermally dimorphic; acute disseminated in young infants (vs asymptomatic adults); H. capsulatum antigen in urine/serum = rapid diagnosis; amphotericin B for disseminated disease
9. Protozoal Infections — Malaria, PJP, Toxoplasmosis
9.1 Congenital Malaria
Epidemiology: worldwide 300–500 million cases/year; US = almost exclusively in infants of foreign-born mothers; P. vivax + P. falciparum = most common congenital species

Clinical: onset average DOL 10–28 (up to 8 weeks); fever, anemia, splenomegaly; 1/3 jaundice; hepatomegaly; irritability, FTT; P. falciparum → hypoglycemia, CNS, splenic rupture, blackwater fever, renal failure
Diagnosis: thin + thick blood smears multiple times; BinaxNOW Malaria rapid test (FDA approved, 15 μL blood) = useful when rapid smear interpretation not available

Treatment: WHO 2022: artemisinin-based combination therapy (ACT) FIRST LINE for uncomplicated + severe malaria in children ≥5 kg; <5 kg: chloroquine/quinine still best; chloroquine-resistant P. falciparum → quinidine gluconate + quinine oral + clindamycin; consult CDC Atlanta for current recommendations
Fetal hemoglobin (HbF) + HbS = protective; maternal antibodies from endemic areas also protective
9.2 Pneumocystis jiroveci Pneumonia (PJP)

PJP ใน Neonates — สำคัญสำหรับ Immunocompromised Infants
Taxonomy: unicellular organism — features of both protozoa and fungi; 3 forms: thick-walled cyst, thin-walled trophozoite, intracystic sporozoite (8/cyst). Sporadic disease: congenital immunodeficiency (SCID) or AIDS — abrupt high fever, coryza, nonproductive cough → rapid dyspnea + cyanosis; CXR: diffuse infiltrates; concurrent CMV >50%. Epidemic infantile PJP (rare in developed countries): slow insidious onset, no fever initially, then tachypnea, cyanosis, sternal retractions. Diagnosis: BAL or open lung biopsy (induced sputum low yield); GMS stain; serologic tests NOT useful. Treatment: TMP-SMX 15–20 mg/kg/day (trimethoprim component) IV divided q6–8h × 3 weeks; use cautiously in young infants (bilirubin displacement from albumin); alternative: pentamidine 4 mg/kg/day IM/IV × 14 days. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX for HIV+ infants ≥2 months old.
9.3 Congenital Toxoplasmosis

Epidemiology: 38% of US pregnant women have IgG (past infection); acute maternal infection 1.1/1000 pregnancies; 1–3/1000 live births in US have Toxoplasma-specific IgM; worldwide 3–8/1000 in utero infections

Transmission: Toxoplasma gondii (obligate intracellular protozoan); cat = definitive host; humans via undercooked meat + oocyst-contaminated food/water/soil; overall maternal-fetal transmission risk: 40% — earlier infection = more severe disease; later infection = higher transmission rate

Congenital Toxoplasmosis — Classic Triad + Clinical Spectrum
Classic triad: hydrocephalus + chorioretinitis + intracranial calcifications — but >75% infected newborns asymptomatic at birth. 4 presentations (McAuley): (1) healthy term with subclinical → symptoms in childhood; (2) healthy term → clinical evidence by few months of life; (3) generalized disease at birth; (4) predominantly neurologic involvement at birth. Subclinical infants: CSF lymphocytic pleocytosis + hypoglycorrhachia + ↑ protein often present despite appearing normal. Continued parasite replication in fetal brain despite maternal control. If untreated: chorioretinitis (most common, often recurrent), seizures, mental retardation, hearing loss emerging in childhood/adulthood. Diagnosis: Toxoplasma-specific IgM, IgA, IgE antibody (confirmatory testing ONLY at Remington Toxoplasma Serology Lab, Palo Alto CA); amniotic fluid PCR (B1 gene) for fetal infection. Workup: MRI brain + CSF evaluation + indirect ophthalmoscopy.
Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid × 1 YEAR for ALL cases (symptomatic + asymptomatic) — AAP Red Book guidelines

Prognosis with treatment: McLeod et al (120 infants, mean 10.5 years follow-up): 100% normal outcome if no substantial neurologic disease at birth + treated 1 year; >72% normal if moderate/severe neurologic disease at birth; no sensorineural hearing loss; BUT 12.6% develop late ocular lesions (up to 12 years after birth) — lifelong ophthalmology follow-up needed
Prevention: France monthly prenatal screening → ↓ transmission rate + ↑ 3-year outcomes; avoid raw meat + cat litter during pregnancy
10. Infection Control Bundle for ICI Prevention
10.1 Antifungal Prophylaxis (Grade A-I)
IV fluconazole 3–6 mg/kg twice weekly for BW <1000 g and/or ≤27 wk GA while IV access present
Nystatin oral/enteral as alternative (Grade A-I) when stable and feeding, but less efficacy + cannot use with GI disease
10.2 Antibiotic + Medication Stewardship (Grade B-II)
Restrict 3rd/4th gen cephalosporins + carbapenems: only for proven gram-negative infections; switch to narrower antibiotics when culture results return
H2 blockers + PPIs: only for proven gastritis; restrict to ≤3 days or until symptoms resolve
Postnatal dexamethasone: only for severe lung disease — strongly avoid for routine use (↑ GI candida growth)
Early enteral feeding: start trophic feeds early; feeds by DOL3 ↓ Candida BSI/meningitis from 8.7% → 3.4% (NICHD NRN)
10.3 CLABSI Bundle (Grade B-II)
Sterile insertion + hub/dressing care; closed medication delivery systems; sterile PN preparation; line changes at bedside with sterile technique
Chitnis et al Pediatrics 2012: Candida CLABSIs ↓ from 0.92 → 0.2/1000 line days (1999–2009) — multi-factorial (prophylaxis + CLABSI bundle + antibiotic stewardship)
10.4 Prenatal + Other Measures
Maternal vaginal candidiasis: screen + treat in high-risk pregnancies (preterm labor, PROM, cervical cerclage) — may ↓ vertical colonization + preterm birth
Lactoferrin: bovine lactoferrin RCT (Manzoni): invasive fungal infections ↓ vs placebo in <1500 g subanalysis — needs confirmation in extremes of prematurity

Probiotics: L. rhamnosus GG + L. reuteri → ↓ gut Candida colonization >50% in <2500 g but no significant ↓ fungal sepsis (underpowered); Cochrane reviews: no demonstrated ↓ late-onset sepsis with probiotics
11. NICU Practical — Bedside Actions
11.1 สิ่งที่ควรทำทันที
Candida BSI → Line removal ASAP: remove CVC ไม่ใช่ replace ทันทีที่ culture positive (biofilm ป้องกัน antifungal penetration); อย่า delay รอดูว่าจะ clear เอง
EOD screen: echo + renal US + HUS + indirect ophthalmoscopy — ทำเลย หรือ 5–7 วันหลัง antifungal; repeat ถ้า persistent candidemia
CCC/cutaneous rash: เริ่ม systemic antifungal ทันทีที่เห็น rash — อย่า delay รอ culture; culture skin (2 sites) + blood + CSF + urine; เลิก humidification ชั่วคราว
Fluconazole prophylaxis: start day 1 of life สำหรับ <1000 g + <28 wk; same days same time (e.g. Tue/Fri 10:00); document start date + planned end date
11.2 สิ่งที่ต้องระวัง
อย่าใช้ topical nystatin เพียงอย่างเดียวสำหรับ CCC: associated with ↑ dissemination; systemic therapy required
อย่าใช้ fluconazole รักษา ICI ถ้าใช้ fluconazole prophylaxis อยู่: resistance pressure; ใช้ amphotericin B แทน
อย่าลืม LP ใน sepsis evaluation: 50% Candida meningitis ไม่มี positive blood culture — วินิจฉัยพลาดถ้าไม่ทำ LP
Mucormycosis → อย่า delay amphotericin B: azoles ไม่ cover mucormycosis; black eschar = emergency → surgical debridement + ampho B
12. Differential Diagnosis — Neonatal Rash + Sepsis

rows: Desquamating/papulopustular rash; white umbilical cord plaques; onset at birth–day 7
Skin culture (2 sites); cord/placenta silver stain; blood+CSF+urine
Systemic antifungal ≥14 days; NOT topical alone
White powder → yellow crust → target lesions; constructional exposure
Culture or biopsy; septate hyphae GMS stain
Black eschar at tape/catheter site; progresses rapidly
Biopsy; non-septate ribbon-like hyphae right-angle branching
Amphotericin B + surgical debridement; NO azoles
Hydrocephalus, chorioretinitis, intracranial calcifications; IUGR, hepatosplenomegaly
Toxo IgM/IgA (Remington Lab); MRI brain; LP; ophthalmology
Benign; eosinophilic pustules on erythematous base; healthy term infant
Clinical; Wright stain of pustule → eosinophils

blocks.text: 13. 5 Bedside Pearls

PEARL 1 — 73% ของ ELBW ที่ติด Candida: ตายหรือ NDI — Prevent, Not Just Treat
Benjamin Pediatrics 2006: ใน ELBW (<1000 g) ที่ไม่ได้รับ prophylaxis — 73% either died or survived with neurodevelopmental impairment (NDI). ICI เป็น preventable cause ของ NDI. Fluconazole prophylaxis 3–6 mg/kg twice weekly เริ่ม day 1 of life ลด ICI ได้ 80–90% + ลด Candida-related mortality 96%. จำ: treat ICI ด้วย amphotericin B ไม่ใช่ fluconazole (เพื่อป้องกัน azole resistance). Early enteral feeding + restrict 3rd-gen ceph + CLABSI bundle = package deal.

PEARL 2 — Line Removal ภายใน 24h: Mortality 21% vs 37%
NICHD NRN (Benjamin 2006): เมื่อ Candida BSI confirmed — remove CVC ภายใน 24h → mortality 21% vs 37% delayed removal (P=0.024). C. parapsilosis: prompt removal → 10% mortality vs 31%. Candida สร้าง biofilm บน catheter → antifungal ไม่ penetrate. อย่า replace, ให้ remove ก่อน และ reinsert หลัง blood cleared (2+ negative cultures after day 4). Persistent candidemia >7 days → add 2nd antifungal + re-evaluate EOD.

PEARL 3 — 50% Candida Meningitis ไม่มี Candidemia — ทำ LP ทุกครั้ง
50% ของ Candida meningitis เกิดโดยไม่มี positive blood culture — ถ้าไม่ทำ LP ใน sepsis evaluation จะ miss diagnosis. CSF cell counts อาจ normal ใน early CNS candidiasis (Candida slow growing + infant immune response อ่อนแอ). ทำ LP ก่อน start antifungal ทุกครั้งที่ evaluate late-onset sepsis. นอกจากนี้: CNS abscess จาก fungal sepsis มักเป็น microscopic → HUS/MRI ทุก case ที่ confirmed ICI ไม่ว่า CSF จะ normal หรือไม่.

PEARL 4 — Mucormycosis Black Eschar = Emergency: NO Azoles
Black eschar ที่ site ของ tape/catheter/covered area ใน NICU preterm = Mucormycosis จนกว่าจะพิสูจน์อย่างอื่น. Mucormycosis RESISTANT to fluconazole และ azoles ทั้งหมด. Treatment: amphotericin B + surgical debridement เร่งด่วน. Morphology: broad NON-SEPTATE ribbon-like hyphae with RIGHT-ANGLE branching (ต่างจาก Aspergillus ที่ septate hyphae). Prevention: manage adhesive tape carefully; no prolonged damp adhesive over skin. Survival improved จาก 18% → 64% post-2000 ด้วย early aggressive treatment.

PEARL 5 — Congenital Toxoplasmosis: 75% Asymptomatic At Birth แต่ Sequelae ตามมา
>75% ของ congenital toxoplasmosis infants ดูปกติตอนแรกเกิด — แต่ถ้าไม่รักษา: chorioretinitis (recurrent), seizures, mental retardation, hearing loss ปรากฏขึ้นในวัยเด็ก/ผู้ใหญ่. McLeod et al: ถ้ารักษา pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid × 1 ปี → 100% normal outcome (ไม่มี substantial neurologic disease at birth). Screening: Toxoplasma IgM/IgA ส่ง Remington Lab เท่านั้น (ไม่ใช่ commercial labs ทั่วไป). Workup ทุก case: MRI brain + CSF + ophthalmology. Ophthalmology follow-up ตลอดชีวิต (12.6% develop late ocular lesions up to 12 years).
14. Evidence Update — Key Studies

Benjamin DK et al Pediatrics 2006;117:84: ELBW ICI outcomes: 73% died or NDI; prompt CVC removal → mortality 21% vs 37%; NDI 57% candidemia vs 29% non-infected; C. albicans mortality 43% vs C. parapsilosis 19%
Benjamin DK et al Pediatrics 2010;126:e865: 128 US NICUs; Candida BSI 7.5% in <1000 g; all ICI burden nearly 2× BSI; mortality BSI 28%, UTI 26%, meningitis/peritonitis 50%, 2+ sites 57%
Kaufman D et al NEJM 2001;345:1660: first fluconazole prophylaxis RCT: 0% vs 20% ICI (P=0.008) in <1000 g; fluconazole 3 mg/kg q48h × 6 weeks

Manzoni P et al NEJM 2007;356:2483: 3-arm multicenter RCT (n=322 <1500 g): fluconazole 3 mg/kg vs 6 mg/kg vs placebo; both doses equally effective; colonization 29.2% → 7.7%/9.8%; ICI 13.2% → 3.8%/2.7%
Ericson JE et al Clin Infect Dis 2016;63:604: patient-level meta-analysis fluconazole prophylaxis: ↓ ICI or death 52%; ↓ ICI 80%; ↓ colonization 72%; 73% of patients <750 g; no resistance emergence
Cleminson J et al Cochrane 2015;CD003850: systematic review antifungal prophylaxis: confirmed efficacy of fluconazole + nystatin; Grade A-I evidence; NNT ~10 for <1000 g all ICIs
Kaufman DA et al J Pediatr 2011;158:759: 8–10 year neurodevelopmental follow-up fluconazole prophylaxis: no differences in Vineland Scales, quality of life, behavior between treated and placebo
Kaufman DA et al Clin Infect Dis 2017;64:1387: CCC outcomes: delaying systemic treatment/using topical alone/treating <10 days → ↑ dissemination; prompt systemic ≥14 days = improved outcomes

Aydemir C et al Arch Dis Child Fetal Neonatal Ed 2011;96:F164: 3-arm RCT fluconazole vs nystatin vs placebo in <1500 g: both reduce ICI vs placebo; similar NEC/mortality between fluconazole 3.2% vs nystatin 4.3%
Ozturk MA et al Mycoses 2006;49:484: nystatin prophylaxis ↓ Candida BSI 90% when started ≤72h vs 62% when started after colonization detected — confirms importance of starting prophylaxis early
Wade KC et al AAC 2008;52:4043: fluconazole PK in 23–40 wk infants: 12 mg/kg/day optimal for treatment pharmacokinetics
Posteraro B et al Pediatr Infect Dis J 2010;29:844: pre-emptive treatment with ET aspirate mannan ≥0.5 ng/mL → ICI ↓ from 75% → 0% in tracheal Candida-positive infants ≤28 wk
Chitnis AS et al Pediatrics 2012;130:e46: Candida CLABSIs ↓ from 0.92 → 0.2/1000 line days 1999–2009 — attributed to antifungal prophylaxis + CLABSI bundles + antibiotic stewardship
Benjamin DK et al PIDJ 2018;37:992: phase 3 RCT micafungin 10 mg/kg vs amphotericin B deoxycholate 1 mg/kg: similar efficacy and safety — micafungin option for BSI treatment

McLeod R et al (Chicago Collaborative Treatment Trial): 120 infants congenital toxoplasmosis, mean 10.5 years follow-up: 100% normal if no neurologic disease at birth + treated 1 year with pyrimethamine+sulfadiazine; >72% normal even with moderate/severe neurologic disease

Sarvikivi E et al J Clin Microbiol 2005;43:2729: 12-year fluconazole prophylaxis NICU: resistant C. parapsilosis emerged ONLY after expanding to whole NICU + ↑ dose to daily 6–12 mg/kg — confirms twice-weekly 3 mg/kg as safer resistance profile
Le J et al AAC 2013;57:2550: each day of candidemia ↑ EOD risk by 10% — early antifungal + line removal critical for limiting dissemination
15. Self-Check Q&A
Q: ICI ใน ELBW ที่ไม่ได้รับ prophylaxis มี incidence เท่าไหร่ และ outcome เป็นอย่างไร?

ตอบ: Incidence: ~10% overall ใน <1000 g (สูงถึง 24% ที่ 23 wk GA) ในกลุ่มไม่รับ prophylaxis (NICHD NRN). Outcome (Benjamin 2006): mortality BSI 28%, UTI 26%, meningitis/peritonitis 50%, 2+ sites 57%; NDI 57% vs 29% non-infected. 73% ของ ELBW ที่ติด Candida: ตายหรือรอดพร้อม NDI — preventable with fluconazole prophylaxis.
Q: Fluconazole prophylaxis ใน NICU: dose, frequency, เมื่อไหร่เริ่ม เมื่อไหร่หยุด?

ตอบ: Dose: 3 or 6 mg/kg IV (equal efficacy); Frequency: twice weekly (same 2 days, same time — e.g. Tue+Fri); Start: day 1 of life สำหรับ <1000 g + <28 wk (starting >72h → 30% less effective); Stop: when IV access (central + peripheral) no longer needed. Treat ICI with amphotericin B (not fluconazole) to avoid resistance pressure.
Q: ทำไม line removal ถึงสำคัญมากใน Candida BSI? หลักฐานคืออะไร?

ตอบ: Biofilm on catheter → antifungal penetration blocked. NICHD NRN: prompt removal <24h → mortality 21% vs 37% (P=0.024). C. parapsilosis: 10% vs 31%. Replace ≠ remove — ต้อง remove ก่อน; new CVC ใส่ได้หลัง 2+ negative cultures after day 4 of treatment. Persistent candidemia >7 days → add 2nd antifungal + re-evaluate EOD.
Q: CCC คืออะไร? ทำไม topical nystatin ถึงไม่เพียงพอ?

ตอบ: CCC: Candida skin infection ตั้งแต่แรกเกิดถึงอาทิตย์แรก — desquamating/papulopustular rash; white umbilical cord plaques. Dissemination risk: term 11%; 1000–2500 g: 33%; <1000 g: 66% — preterm มี risk สูงมากที่จะ disseminate จาก skin → blood → CNS. Topical alone → associated with ↑ dissemination (Kaufman 2017). ต้องรักษา: systemic antifungal ≥14 days เริ่มทันทีที่เห็น rash; culture ก่อน start แต่ไม่ต้องรอผล.
Q: Congenital toxoplasmosis: infant ที่ดูปกติ ต้องทำอะไรบ้าง?

ตอบ: >75% asymptomatic ตอนแรกเกิด — ถ้าไม่รักษา: chorioretinitis (recurrent), seizures, NDI, hearing loss ปรากฏในวัยเด็ก. Workup: Toxoplasma IgM/IgA (Remington Lab Palo Alto CA เท่านั้น); MRI brain; LP + CSF; indirect ophthalmoscopy. Treatment: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + folinic acid × 1 year สำหรับ ALL cases. McLeod et al: 100% normal ถ้า no significant neurologic disease + treated 1 year. Lifelong ophthalmology follow-up needed (12.6% develop late ocular lesions).
Q: Amphotericin B deoxycholate vs lipid formulations — เลือกอะไรใน NICU?

ตอบ: Amphotericin B deoxycholate: FIRST CHOICE (AAP Red Book 2021) — dose 1 mg/kg; excellent coverage almost all Candida; low cost; safe in neonates (no test dose needed; nephrotoxicity rare in neonates vs adults); preferred for UTI/renal candidiasis (↑ kidney tissue concentration). Lipid preparations (5–7 mg/kg): use when ampho B deoxycholate not tolerated or in concern for peripheral access phlebitis; 95% eradication; higher cost. No RCT showing superiority over deoxycholate in neonates.
Q: Mucormycosis ใน NICU: presentation, diagnosis, treatment ที่แตกต่างจาก Aspergillus?

ตอบ: Presentation: black eschar ที่ site ของ adhesive tape/catheter/covered area — progresses rapidly to necrosis. Hyphae (biopsy/culture): broad NON-SEPTATE ribbon-like, RIGHT-ANGLE branching (vs Aspergillus: septate, dichotomous branching). CRITICAL: Mucormycosis RESISTANT to azoles (fluconazole, voriconazole). Treatment: amphotericin B + surgical debridement; no azoles. Prevention: manage adhesive tape carefully.
Q: BDG (Beta-D-Glucan) ใน neonates: ตีความอย่างไร และข้อจำกัดคืออะไร?

ตอบ: Cutoff: >125 pg/mL ใน neonates (vs >80 pg/mL adults) — ใน neonates colonization + CoNS infections ↑ BDG; infected: 364 pg/mL vs non-infected 89 pg/mL. ข้อจำกัด: (1) false positive post-transfusion (PRBC/FFP → BDG 170 vs 57 pg/mL); (2) colonization alone อาจ ↑ BDG = ICI level; (3) not yet replacing cultures. Usefulness: guide empiric therapy + monitor treatment response (levels ↓ with effective therapy).

Day 149 Preview — Fanaroff Ch.52
บทต่อไป: Chapter 52 — Viral Infections in the Neonate. เตรียมตัว: congenital CMV, HSV neonatal, enterovirus, RSV, congenital rubella, TORCH infections; CMV: most common congenital viral infection (0.5–1% live births); HSV: neonatal (SEM vs CNS vs disseminated), acyclovir treatment + prophylaxis; enterovirus: summer/fall seasonality, myocarditis in neonates; RSV: bronchiolitis seasonality, palivizumab prophylaxis; congenital rubella syndrome (now rare); COVID-19 perinatal outcomes.
